narrative_blocks.header: Fifty years at Charles Hart Company Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: James Woodbridge Hale spent fifty years employed by the Charles Hart Co. of Hartford CT — a half-century at one Hartford firm. He attended Hopkins Grammar School in his youth, the same institution his older brother Dr. Charles Frederick Hale had prepared at decades earlier. He and his wife Olive Post Rockwell Hale were among the oldest members of Hartford's Center Congregational Church.

narrative_blocks.body: James Woodbridge Hale spent fifty years employed by the Charles Hart Co. of Hartford CT — a half-century at one Hartford firm. He attended Hopkins Grammar School in his youth, the same institution his older brother Dr. Charles Frederick Hale had prepared at decades earlier. He and his wife Olive Post Rockwell Hale were among the oldest members of Hartford's Center Congregational Church.

bio.extra_names: Olive Post (Stiles book rendering, possibly previous-marriage surname or family naming pattern)

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with husband James Woodbridge Hale who died 4 months earlier.

parents.father_research_notes: Civil War US Navy officer; see X02822 easter_egg entry.
parents.mother_research_notes: Ruama M. 'Emma' Ayer Rockwell (1822-1847) — died when Olive was 5. Not built per scope (Capt. John's other children/spouse not documented for the Hooker tree).

narrative_blocks.header: She died four months after her husband
narrative_blocks.body: Olive Rockwell married James Woodbridge Hale in 1866 and outlived him by only four months, dying at Hartford in 1921. She had lost her own mother at the age of five.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with parents.

naming_inspiration.description: Middle name 'Stiles' carries her father James Woodbridge Hale's mother's maiden surname — Julia Stiles Hale (HD4160). The Stiles name persists into gen 8 of this Hale line.

narrative_blocks.header: Society of Founders of Hartford, never married
narrative_blocks.body: Edith Stiles Hale was a member of the Society of the Descendants of the Founders of Hartford — an institutional recognition of her descent from the founding generation, the same Hooker ancestry that organizes this genealogical project a century later. She never married, lived 75-76 years (1874-1950), and was buried at Old North Cemetery Hartford with her parents.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with wife Julia Stiles Hale and children.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Glastonbury CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Glastonbury CT, maiden name Stevens. Not in JSON.

burial.plot_notes: Old North Cemetery, Hartford CT — buried with husband Frederick Augustus Hale and several children including James Woodbridge Hale, Olive Rockwell Hale, and Edith Stiles Hale.

narrative_blocks.header: Married thirty, lost three of six children
narrative_blocks.body: Julia Stiles married Frederick A Hale of Glastonbury CT on April 7, 1825 at age 30 — late by the era's standards. She bore six children over the next fourteen years; three of them died young (an infant daughter in 1826, James at 10 months in 1830, Alfred at 7 months in 1832). The three who survived to adulthood were Charles Frederick Hale MD (1827, Panama Railroad chief medical officer), James Woodbridge Hale (1833), and Cornelius Stiles Hale (1839, died San Antonio TX 1876 unmarried).

Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry [-34] of Job Stiles

career.notes: Shoemaker by trade (EH 462 + Stiles book). The Stiles family resided in Enfield CT for about ten years (c.1810-1821) where Job died in 1813, then his widow returned to Windsor.

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — buried 'on one stone' with wife Mary Drake Stiles.

narrative_blocks.header: Windsor shoemaker, brother of silversmith Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: Job Stiles was a shoemaker by trade, working in Windsor CT and later Enfield CT. His older brother Samuel Stiles was a Northampton silversmith and clockmaker — together they represent two New England craft trades within the same Stiles family generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-eight, buried with Mary in Windsor
narrative_blocks.body: Job died at Windsor CT on April 13, 1813 at age 48, during the family's roughly decade-long Enfield CT residence (c.1810-1821). His widow Mary Drake Stiles survived him by twenty-six years, dying March 14, 1839 at age 77. They were buried together in the Old Windsor Graveyard with their names inscribed on a single stone.

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — 'on one stone' with husband Job Stiles.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Windsor CT, of the Griswold family — middle name of grandson Ezra Griswold Stiles (HD4158) carries this lineage. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-six years widowed, buried three children
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Drake Stiles was widowed at fifty-one in 1813 when her husband Job died. She lived another twenty-six years in Windsor, dying at seventy-seven in 1839. She buried three of her nine children: infant Mary in September 1786 (age 5 months), Laura in September 1810 (age 21), and Ezra Griswold lost at sea around 1819 (presumed dead at 28).

INST001.name: Hopkins Grammar School
INST001.notes:
Founded by bequest of Edward Hopkins, Governor of Connecticut and overseer of Thomas Hooker's will. Hopkins also named Mary Newton (H00003) and Sarah Wilson (H00006) in his own will with direct bequests.
[founder] Edward Hopkins
